danger_signals.visual_form: Mechanical hostile units visible in level spaces
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat to player progression; Dr. Proton's invasion force

danger_signals.visual_form: On-screen boxes, barriers, and environmental hazards that can be shot
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental dangers requiring destruction or avoidance to proceed

danger_signals.visual_form: Final episode levels lack visible exit point
danger_signals.meaning: Boss encounter imminent; Dr. Proton confrontation required instead of standard level completion

danger_signals.visual_form: Destructible surveillance devices placed throughout levels
danger_signals.meaning: Dr. Proton's surveillance infrastructure; targets for bonus achievement

safety_signals.meaning: Restores player vitality; indicates safe resource availability
Enhanced offensive capability; progression safety marker

safety_signals.visual_form: Goal location at end of standard levels (absent only on boss levels)
safety_signals.meaning: Safe completion state; passage to next level available
Tool availability for problem-solving and hazard mitigation

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Multiple Techbot unit types present throughout invasion
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Devastated Los Angeles cityscape (Episode 1); Dr. Proton's moonbase (Episode 2); futuristic time-displaced zones (Episode 3)

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Duke Nukem character sprite as opposition to Techbot aesthetic
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Ruined urban environments showing aftermath of invasion; moonbase infiltration spaces; time-fractured environments

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Los Angeles setting establishes Earth as target of Dr. Proton's conquest
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Techbots actively occupying and controlling urban spaces; Duke must navigate hostile territory

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Devastated city environments with destructible urban elements; Techbot presence throughout
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies actively patrolling; obstacles block standard progression routes

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Moonbase architecture replacing Earth cityscape; shift to off-world environment
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Techbot concentration increases; Dr. Proton's operational center requires direct confrontation

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Futuristic time-displaced zones; visual shift from present-day settings
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Dr. Proton uses time machinery to escape; Duke pursues across temporal boundaries

environmental_storytelling.example: Three-episode structure uses location shifts to communicate escalating stakes and Dr. Proton's desperation
Cameras and obstacles visible in levels demonstrate Dr. Proton's infrastructure; destroying them shows player agency against invasion
Seven 10,000-point bonuses per level (e.g., destroying all cameras) reinforce player's tactical disruption of enemy operations
Final level of each episode lacks exit, instead requiring Dr. Proton defeat—signals world-state dependency on boss confrontation

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Hostile patrol and engagement in level spaces; occupying human environments
Episode 1: directing invasion from distance; Episode 2: retreats to moonbase; Episode 3: activates time machine to escape temporal boundaries
Pursues through three distinct geographic/temporal domains; destruction of enemy infrastructure and confrontation with final boss
Remain destructible; clearing them enables player progression and contributes to achievement goals

notes: Duke Nukem (1991) communicates world-state primarily through environmental setting progression rather than dynamic visual state changes. The three-episode structure (Los Angeles invasion → moonbase infiltration → temporal pursuit) serves as the primary manifestation vehicle for world-state evolution. Danger signals are relatively minimal—primarily enemy presence and structural obstacles. Safety signals manifest as collectible power-ups and level exits. No faction color-coding or symbolic identity systems are documented; factions are distinguished by character design (humanoid Duke vs. mechanical Techbots) and environmental occupation. The absence of level exits on boss-final-levels provides explicit visual communication that world-state has shifted to require confrontation rather than navigation.
